query: is ACE2 expressed on cell surfaces

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Receptors -- Clinical Significance -- Infectious Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: The influenza virus infects epithelial cells in the upper and lower respiratory tracts. The viral surface protein hemagglutinin binds to sialic acid, a sugar found on the cell surface. The influenza virus binds to sialic acid, which acts as a cell receptor necessary for the virus to infect the cell. [28] Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2), the virus responsible for COVID-19, binds to angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) and the type II transmembrane serine protease (TMPRSS2) coreceptor to enter host cells. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Features, Evaluation, and Treatment of Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: SARS-CoV-2 gains entry into the host cells by binding the SARS-CoV-2 spike or S protein (S1) to the ACE2 receptors in the respiratory epithelium. ACE2 receptors are also expressed by other organs such as the upper esophagus, enterocytes from the ileum, myocardial cells, proximal tubular cells of the kidney, and urothelial cells of the bladder. [47] The viral attachment process is followed by priming the spike protein S2 subunit by the host transmembrane serine protease 2 (TMPRSS2) that facilitates cell entry and subsequent viral replication. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Bamlanivimab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: SARS-CoV-2 is a single-stranded enveloped RNA virus. The outer surface comprises the envelope, membrane, and many glycosylated S-proteins. The virus binds to the host cell's angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE-2) receptor via S-protein. The S-protein comprises 2 subunits, S1 and S2. With the help of the receptor-binding domain (RBD) of the S1 subunit, the virus binds to the ACE-2 receptor found in cells of the respiratory system, gastrointestinal system, and endothelium. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: SARS-CoV-2 gains entry into the hosts' cells by binding the SARS-CoV-2 spike or S protein (S1) to the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptors abundantly on respiratory epithelium such as type II alveolar epithelial cells. Besides the respiratory epithelium, ACE2 receptors are also expressed by other organs, such as the upper esophagus, enterocytes from the ileum, myocardial cells, proximal tubular cells of the kidney, and urothelial cells of the bladder. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Etesevimab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Many glycosylated S proteins layer the surface of SARS-CoV-2, helping the virus bind to the host cell's ACE2 (Angiotensin-converting enzyme-2 receptor) and facilitating viral entry. The S protein has 2 subunits: S1 and S2. The S1 subunit comprises NTD (N-terminal domain) and RBD (receptor-binding domain). [7] The RBD of the S1 subunit binds to the host cell's ACE2. The RBD is a primary target for producing neutralizing monoclonal antibodies. Etesevimab, bamlanivimab, casirivimab, and imdevimab are neutralizing monoclonal antibodies available against SARS-COV-2. Etesevimab and bamlanivimab bind to the overlapping epitopes in RBD (see Figures. Mechanism of Action of Etesevimab, Bamlanivimab, Mechanism of Action of Etesevimab). Casirivimab and imdevimab bind to non-overlapping epitopes of RBD. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The SARS coronavirus (SARS-CoV) uses the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptor for entry into its host. [4] SARS-CoV recognizes the host ACE2 receptor through the virus's receptor-binding domain. Mutation in this domain may allow this and other coronaviruses to cause new and increased cross-species infections. [11] The virus concentrates in the lungs and small bowel, which are areas with a high density of its receptors. [4] Specifically, the alveolar epithelium shows the highest focus of infection. [4] The infection leads to serous pleural effusions, pulmonary edema, and consolidations with relative sparing of the upper respiratory tract. [4] The lung injury is thought to be due to an out-of-control immune response by the host leading to excess quantities of pro-inflammatory cytokines. [4] [12] Some patients will develop bacterial (i.e., Staphylococcus aureus, Strenotrophomonas maltophilia ), viral, or fungal (i.e., Aspergillus , Candida ) superinfections. [4] The histopathology features, which have been found during postmortem and may not be representative of all SARS cases, show epithelial cell proliferation, diffuse alveolar damage, and macrophage infiltration of the lung. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Providing Access To Monoclonal Antibody Treatment Of Coronavirus (COVID-19) Patients In Rural And Underserved Areas (Archived) -- Function -- Mechanism of Action of Monoclonal Antibodies Against SARS-CoV-2
retrieved_docs.content: The pathophysiology of COVID-19 is described by the entry of SARS-CoV-2, the causative virus, into the hosts' cells by binding the SARS-CoV-2 spike or S protein (S1) to the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptors expressed abundantly on the respiratory epithelium, such as type II alveolar epithelial cells. This viral entry is mediated by the receptor-binding domain (RBD) on the spike protein followed by priming of the spike protein (S2) by the host transmembrane serine protease 2 (TMPRSS2) that facilitates cell entry and subsequent viral replication. [12] Monoclonal antibodies prevent the viral attachment by binding to a non-overlapping epitope on the surface spike protein RBD of SARS-CoV-2 with high affinity, thereby blocking the binding of the virus to the human ACE2 receptor.

retrieved_docs.title: Best Practices For Administering Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus (COVID-19) (Archived) -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Monoclonal antibodies against COVID-19 disrupt host cell entry by binding the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (AE2) receptor binding domain (RBD) of the virus. SARS-CoV-2 engages the host cell ACE2 receptor via the spike (S) glycoprotein to cause infection. This makes spike S glycoprotein on the coronavirus surface a common target for antiviral therapy, most notable of which are monoclonal antibodies. [13]

retrieved_docs.content: This enzyme is expressed on plasma membranes of vascular endothelial cells, primarily in the pulmonary circulation. [14] It cleaves the 2 amino acids from the C-terminal of angiotensin I to make the peptide angiotensin II.

retrieved_docs.title: Mechanical Ventilation and Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation Considerations in COVID-19 -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The SARS-CoV-2 virus is a single-stranded RNA virus belonging to the Betacoronavirus genus and consists of four major structural proteins identified in the pathogenesis of COVID-19 disease. [1] The spike protein (S) is considered the main structural protein that protrudes from the viral surface and gives it a “corona-like" appearance. The S protein binds to the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE 2) receptors of the human cells leading to endocytosis of the virus. Other proteins include the membrane (M) protein, envelope (E) protein, and nucleocapsid (N) protein that plays a significant role in infectivity and viral replication. [2] The ACE 2 receptors are expressed highly in the nasal, pharyngeal, and bronchial mucosa and are the primary source of entry for the virus. [3] Other mucosal surfaces such as the gastrointestinal tract, kidneys, and conjunctiva also express ACE 2 receptors and have been found to be affected by the SARS-CoV-2 virus.

retrieved_docs.title: Ophthalmic Manifestations of Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathogenesis and tissue tropism of SARS-CoV-2 are linked to the interaction between the viral spike protein and its cognate receptor on human host cells— the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE-2) receptor. Efficient cell entry requires cleavage by the protein transmembrane serine protease 2 (TMPRSS2). ACE-2 expression is predominant in respiratory mucosal and alveolar epithelial cells but has also been identified in other tissues, including the gastrointestinal tract, kidney, vascular endothelial cells, immune cells, and even neurons. Virulence is achieved through direct cellular invasion and death and the induction of widespread cytokine-driven inflammation and vascular leakage. [15] Additionally, immune cell and complement debris may contribute to an increased thromboembolic state.

retrieved_docs.title: Gastrointestinal and Hepatic Manifestations of Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The pathogenesis of GI tract involvement in SARS-CoV-2 is likely multifactorial. Several hypotheses have been described, including direct angiotensin-converting enzyme 2-mediated viral cytotoxicity of the intestinal mucosa, cytokine-induced inflammation, gut dysbiosis, and vascular abnormalities. SARS-CoV-2 gains entry into the host cells by binding the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein to the angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2) receptors present on the respiratory epithelium, followed by priming of the spike protein by the host transmembrane serine protease 2 (TMPRSS2) that facilitates cell entry and subsequent viral replication. [9] ACE2 receptor is abundantly expressed in the enterocytes of the GI tract as well, mainly in the GI tract epithelial cells, and human organoid models have demonstrated active viral replication. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Benefits and Risks of Administering Monoclonal Antibody Therapy for Coronavirus (COVID-19) (Archived) -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: The novel coronavirus, SARS-CoV-2, is a positive-sense single-stranded RNA virus spread through respiratory droplets. [9] The virus gains entry into the cell by binding its spike protein to the angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) 2 receptors on host cells. [10] This receptor is found on the respiratory epithelium, upper esophagus, ileum, myocardium, proximal tubular cells in the kidney, and the urothelium of the bladder. After binding the ACE2 receptor, the virus can enter the cell, and viral replication can occur. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1836
retrieved_docs.content: Renin‑inhibitors (function) describe the pharmacologic action of blocking the enzyme renin, a pivotal component of the renin‑angiotensin‑aldosterone system that regulates blood pressure and fluid balance. Remikiren is a specific drug that exemplifies this function, being a member of the renin‑inhibitor class. By inhibiting renin, remikiren reduces the conversion of angiotensinogen to angiotensin I, thereby decreasing downstream angiotensin II formation and contributing to blood pressure control. This relationship underscores that remikiren operates through the renin‑inhibitor mechanism, illustrating how individual agents can embody broader therapeutic functions within the renin‑inhibitor drug category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2473
retrieved_docs.content: Rescinnamine achieves its therapeutic effect by employing the mechanism of action typical of angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors; specifically, it inhibits the angiotensin‑converting enzyme, which leads to reduced formation of angiotensin II, vasodilation, and subsequent lowering of blood pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2782
retrieved_docs.content: Candesartan, eprosartan and valsartan are all classified as plain angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs). As members of this drug class, they selectively inhibit the angiotensin II type 1 receptor, which results in vasodilation, reduced aldosterone secretion, and consequently lower blood pressure. These ARBs are commonly employed in the management of hypertension, heart failure, and diabetic nephropathy, and their shared mechanism of action underlies their therapeutic use in cardiovascular and renal protection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 4 -- Community 2952
retrieved_docs.content: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), plain constitute a pharmacologic class that includes the individual agents eprosartan, irbesartan, and olmesartan medoxomil. Each of these medications is a member of the plain ARB category and functions by selectively blocking the angiotensin II type 1 receptor, thereby inhibiting the vasoconstrictive and aldosterone‑secreting effects of angiotensin II. By reducing systemic vascular resistance, these agents are employed to manage hypertension and related cardiovascular conditions, offering therapeutic options that share a common mechanism of action while differing in pharmacokinetic profiles and dosing characteristics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1384
retrieved_docs.content: Angiotensin II is classified as a polypeptide hormone and its principal physiologic effect is vasoconstriction, a response that plays a key role in the regulation of blood pressure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2524
retrieved_docs.content: AGENTS ACTING ON THE RENIN‑ANGIOTENSIN SYSTEM include a subclass called ANGIOTENSIN II RECEPTOR BLOCKERS (ARBs), PLAIN, which are medications that block the effects of angiotensin II and thereby modulate the renin‑angiotensin system. Specific agents that belong to the ANGIOTENSIN II RECEPTOR BLOCKERS (ARBs), PLAIN class are eprosartan, telmisartan, and valsartan. These drugs are recognized as individual members of the ARB group and share the common mechanism of antagonizing angiotensin II receptors to lower blood pressure and provide cardiovascular protection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2226
retrieved_docs.content: Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), referred to as “angiotensin ii receptor blockers (arbs), plain,” are classified as agents acting on the renin‑angiotensin system. Within this pharmacologic class, individual drugs such as irbesartan and telmisartan are recognized as members of the ARB group, sharing the common mechanism of blocking the angiotensin II receptor to modulate blood pressure and cardiovascular function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1986
retrieved_docs.content: Agents acting on the renin‑angiotensin system encompass the class of angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), plain, which are non‑combination medications that inhibit the effects of angiotensin II. Specific drugs that belong to this ARB class include eprosartan, irbesartan, and valsartan. Recognizing that eprosartan, irbesartan and valsartan are members of the angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs), plain category clarifies their shared mechanism of blocking angiotensin II receptors, a key therapeutic approach for controlling hypertension and protecting cardiovascular health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1145
retrieved_docs.content: 1H‑tetrazole is a chemical scaffold that is present in both cilostazol and aliskiren, linking these two drugs through a common structural motif. The entities amidy and azoly are also related to cilostazol as substructures, and azoly is similarly connected to aliskiren, while amidy is mapped to aliskiren, indicating a close chemical relationship between amidy and the renin‑inhibitor aliskiren. Both cilostazol and aliskiren share a contraindication in patients with فرط تحسس (hypersensitivity), meaning that use of either drug should be avoided in individuals who have a known hypersensitivity reaction. The class Angiotensin‑Converting Enzyme Inhibitors [MoA] is characterized by the mechanism of action exemplified by rescinnamine, and this mechanism is contraindicated when combined with the mechanism of aliskiren. Consequently, co‑administration of aliskiren with ACE‑inhibitor therapy requires caution because the pharmacologic actions of aliskiren are opposed by the ACE‑inhibitor mechanism. Together, these relationships highlight important structural commonalities, shared hypersensitivity contraindications, and drug‑interaction considerations that are relevant for safe patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 281
retrieved_docs.content: Rimantadine is classified as a cyclic amine and a direct‑acting antiviral, placing it within a group of compounds that target viral replication mechanisms. A notable physiologic effect of rimantadine is the alteration of cellular synthetic activity, which can influence both viral production and host cell metabolic processes. Recognizing rimantadine’s membership in the cyclic amine, direct‑acting antiviral class and its capacity to modify cellular synthesis helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, anticipate potential cellular effects, and optimize its use in antiviral therapy.
